segments.s744-g33: This is a list introduced by the prepositional particle ز (from). The sequence names birds of prey: شاهین (shaahin) 'hawk/falcon' followed by وز which here is a contracted combination (و + ز) meaning 'and from' — the provided lemma senses don't list that usage, so treat it as a connective. باز is the noun 'hawk' (another species) and پران is an adjective 'winged/feathered' qualifying عقاب 'eagle'. Word order is noun phrases linked by (implicit) and/from; no finite verb appears in this clause.
segments.s744-g34: Another coordinated prepositional list begun by ز 'from'. It enumerates beasts: شیر 'lion', پلنگ 'leopard', and نهنگ 'whale', followed by the locative phrase اندر آب 'in the water' (اندر = in/within, آب = water). The coordinating و is the simple 'and'.
segments.s744-g35: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject (or topic). برگزیدند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they chose/accepted'. پیمان 'covenant/pledge' is the object and اوی is the old/poetic possessive pronoun 'his' (attached after the noun). The verb precedes the object here, a common Persian word order.
segments.s744-g36: چو 'like/as' introduces a simile: خورشید روشن 'the bright sun'. بدی in this line functions as an archaic/persianized past copula (here effectively 'was' or 'shone'); the provided lemma senses do not list that verbal sense so it is mapped as -1. جان اوی 'his soul' is the predicate noun with اوی = 'his'. The clause reads 'his soul was (as) the bright sun.'
segments.s744-g37: نخستین 'the first (one)' is a nominal head; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بنهاد is a past, 3rd person singular verb 'he placed/established'. گنج عروس is a genitive construction 'the bride's treasure' with the possessed noun following the possessor.
segments.s744-g38: A continued list introduced by the preposition ز 'from': ز چین 'from China' then و ز برطاس 'and from a wineskin/container' — note وز before روم is again the contracted 'و + ز' (and from). The sequence finishes with روم 'Rome/Byzantium' و روس 'and Rus'. Each geographic/name item is coordinated with و or the contracted وز.
segments.s744-g39: دگر 'another/other' modifies گنج 'treasure'. پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' qualifying the treasure. در خوشاب is a locative phrase: در 'in' + خوشاب 'pleasant water/sweet spring' (a poetic term for a pleasant pool or wine). بود is the past copula 'was'. The overall order is demonstrative/adjective + noun + locative phrase + past verb.
segments.s744-g40: که 'that/which' introduces a relative clause referring back to the treasure. بالاش is بالا 'above' + enclitic pronoun -ش 'its/it', meaning 'above it' or 'on top of it'. یک تیر پرتاب is 'one thrown arrow' or 'a single projectile' (تیر 'arrow/bolt', پرتاب 'a throw/projection' used as noun). بود is the past copula 'was' describing existence/location.
segments.s744-g73: 'که' here is a subordinating conjunction introducing the clause ('as' / 'that'); 'نیک و بد' are coordinated nouns 'good and bad' serving as the clause's subject; 'اندر جهان' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' placed before the verb; 'بگذرد' is a third-person present (classical/poetic) verb 'to pass' (verb-final order typical of Persian).
segments.s744-g74: 'زمانه' is the subject meaning 'Time' or 'fate'; 'دم ما' is a direct object/phrase 'our breath' (literally) or 'our moments'; the verb 'همی‌بشمرد' has the prefixed archaic/habitual marker 'همی-' plus present 'بشمرد' meaning 'counts' or 'reckons' — the clause is SOV with the continuous/present sense given by 'همی-'.
segments.s744-g75: This is a pair of parallel conditional clauses introduced by repeated 'اگر' ('if'); 'تخت یابی' is 'you obtain a throne' (object before verb, verb 'یابی' in the present/subjunctive sense 'you attain'); the second 'اگر' clause omits the verb by ellipsis (i.e. 'if (you) attain a crown and treasure'), a common poetic economy.
segments.s744-g76: 'وگر' is another conditional 'and if / but if'; 'چند' here is an adverbial 'for a while / some (time)'; 'پوینده' is a predicative noun 'a seeker' and 'باشی' is the present/subjunctive 'you are' used in the protasis ('if you are'); 'به رنج' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/with hardship' modifying the state 'باشی'.
segments.s744-g77: 'سرانجام' is an adverb 'in the end / finally'; 'جای تو' is the possessional noun phrase 'your place'; 'خاکست و خشت' are coordinated nouns 'ashes and brick' serving as the nominal predicate — the copula is omitted, as is normal in Persian poetry, so the line reads 'your place (is) ashes and brick.'
segments.s744-g78: 'جز از تخم نیکی' is a restrictive prepositional phrase 'except/from the seed of goodness'; 'نبایدت' is an older negative-modal construction with the enclitic '-ت' addressing 'you' and means 'you must not / you should not' (here used to express moral injunction); 'کشت' is the verb 'to sow/plant' (used as the action governed by the negative), so the whole clause means 'you should plant nothing except the seed of goodness.'
segments.s744-g9: مبادا ... باشی is a negative wish/command structure: مبادا (an optative/negative particle) + که (clause marker) + باشی (present subjunctive of بودن) yields 'may it not be that you are...' or more idiomatically 'do not be...'. گستاخ is the predicate adjective 'insolent' and به دهر is a prepositional phrase 'toward the world/fate' modifying گستاخ باشی.
segments.s744-g10: که introduces the explanatory/casual clause 'for/because'; the clause has a somewhat compressed, poetic word order: از پای زهرش فزونست زهر — از پای (from the root/foot of) + زهرش (its poison) is the source, فزونست is an archaic fused form of 'فزون است' (third person singular present: 'is more/greater'), and the final زهر repeats the noun for emphasis ('poison'). Grammatically, this is a causal clause explaining why one must not be insolent.
segments.s744-g11: This phrase uses repeated با ('with') to coordinate two instruments/qualities: با آز و با کینه = 'with greed and with hatred/malice.' مساایچ is an obscure/archaic item (here left untranslated as an adverbial/particle introducing prohibition or emphasis); دست functions as the object 'hand' or figuratively 'control/claim' which these passions would effect.
segments.s744-g12: ز منزل مکن جایگاه نشست is an imperative-negative construction: ز (poetic 'from'/'away from') منزل (dwelling/station) + مکن (negative imperative 'do not do') governs جایگاه نشست ('to make a seat/place of settlement' or 'to settle as your place'). نشسـت here is the verbal noun/verb 'to sit/settle.' The whole segment means 'do not, from a dwelling, make it your place of settlement' — i.e. do not seize/settle a house through greed and hatred.
segments.s744-g13: سرای سپنجست is a nominal phrase with an obscure predicate: سرای 'house/household' (here used as subject) + سپنجست (an uncertain/obscure adjective or epithet). با راه و رو is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to its way and appearance' (با + two nouns coordinated by و), modifying or specifying in what respect the house is characterized.
segments.s744-g14: تو is the addressed 'you' (subject). گردی کهن here is a verbless/nominal predicate: گردی (a noun that can mean 'dust' or 'wandering') modified by کهن 'old/ancient' — 'you are old/worn.' دیگر آرند نو is a short clause where آرند is an archaic/persianized present plural of آوردن ('they bring'), with دیگر 'another/other' and نو 'new' as its object/compliment: 'others bring the new.' The line thus contrasts the addressee's oldness with newcomers who bring the new.
segments.s744-g15: This is a paratactic couplet: یکی (one/someone) + اندر آید (comes in) — simple subject + verb order — then contrasted with دگر بگذرد (another passes by). دگر is 'another' and بگذرد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of گذشتن here with the sense 'passes (away/past).' The line expresses the flow of people: one comes, another goes.
segments.s744-g16: زمانی ('for a time/for a while') sets the temporal frame; به منزل is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the lodging/stage.' چمد‌گر (چمدَگر) is 'the porter' (one who handles trunks) and چرد is a verbal form meaning literally 'chews' or 'grazes' (here used idiomatically to mean 'pauses/rests/feeds'). Grammatically, the subject (porter) follows the locative phrase, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s744-g1: کنون is an adverb placed clause-initially meaning 'now'. از introduces the topic 'of/concerning' and takes بزرگی (a noun) as its object; خسرو follows بزرگی in a genitive-like phrase (‘the greatness of Khosrow’). سخن at the end names the action/topic ('speech/tale') and functions as the direct object/topic of بگویم in the next segment.
segments.s744-g2: بگویم is a 1st-person subjunctive/optative form ('let me tell' / 'I will tell') used here to introduce narration; کنم follows as a light verb/auxiliary 'to do/make' giving an emphatic or causative sense ('I will cause/do [this]'). تازه modifies روز (adjective before noun in Persian), and روز کهن = 'the old day/ancient time' is a poetic phrase meaning 'the ancient tale' which the speaker says he will 'renew' (کنم تازه).
segments.s744-g3: بران سان is a fixed demonstrative-qualifier meaning 'of that sort/so great' (بران + سان = 'such'). بزرگی is the head noun 'greatness'. کس here is an indefinite 'anyone/no one' depending on the negative that follows; اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' placed after کس (typical classical word order: qualifier + noun + person + location).
segments.s744-g4: ندارد is the 3rd-person singular present of داشتن 'to have' used here impersonally ('there is not / no one has'). بیاد functions as a noun/adverbial 'in memory / remembrance' (بیاد داشتن = 'to be remembered' or 'to have remembrance'), and از marks the source 'from'; کهان here means 'ancient times', while مهان is contextually 'the great/illustrious ones' (poetic usage). The whole phrase says that no one has such greatness remembered from antiquity and among eminent people.
segments.s744-g5: هر آنکس که is a standard relative/indefinite 'whoever/anyone who'. او is the emphatic third-person pronoun 'he' (referring back to the subject of the preceding lines). دفتر شاه is a genitive phrase 'the king's book' (دفتر = book/record, شاه = king). خواند is the 3rd-person singular past form but in epic contexts it can be a general/presentive 'reads/recites'—here it introduces the condition 'whoever reads'.
segments.s744-g6: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. گیتیش is گیتی + the enclitic -ش 'his' = 'his world/realm' (referring back to the king). دامن ('hem/skirt') is the object; بباید is the present modal 'must/ought' and فشاند is the verbal stem 'scatter/throw' — together 'بباید فشاند' = 'one must scatter/throw' (i.e., show obeisance by spreading/throwing the hem).
segments.s744-g7: سزد‌گر is a conditional/formulaic adverbial phrase 'if it be fitting' (literally 'deeming-fit' or 'should it be fitting'). بگویم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'let me tell / I may tell' and introduces the speaker's intended action. یکی داستان is the direct object 'one tale/one story'.
segments.s744-g8: که here is a complementizer 'that'. باشد is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive of بودن 'to be' ('may be / might be'), used optatively. خردمند is 'a wise person', and هم‌داستان is an adjective/noun meaning 'of the same mind / like‑minded' (i.e., may a wise man agree with the tale).
segments.s744-g41: که is the relative 'which/that' introducing a clause about the treasure. خضرا (here a proper name) + نهادند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb ‘they set/placed’ used idiomatically for ‘they made / they called’; نامش combines نام 'name' with the enclitic ش 'its' to mark the thing being named; ردان here is the name assigned (functioning as a noun). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object in an older/poetic register, with the verb نهادند marking the action of naming.
segments.s744-g42: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same'; تازیان is the plural noun 'Arabs' modified by نامور 'renowned' (an adjective placed after the noun in this poetic word order); بخردان here describes them as 'among the wise' or 'wise ones' (an adjectival/epithet form). The whole short phrase identifies the people being referred to.
segments.s744-g43: دگر means 'another' (introducing a different treasure). گنج is 'treasure' (the noun). باد‌آورش is a compound name: باد 'wind' + آورش (here functioning as a nominalizing/'bringer' element), so together 'Wind‑Bringer' — the phrase is what they 'خواندند' (past plural 'they called/named'). خواندند is the past-tense verb agreeing with the plural agent.
segments.s744-g44: شمارش بکردند is a two-part verbal phrase: شمارش 'the counting/reckoning' as a noun + بکردند (ب‑ + کردند) 'they did', so 'they counted' (literally 'they did the counting'). و is the coordinating 'and', در is locative 'inside', and ماندند is past plural 'they remained' or 'they stayed', so the clause says they performed the count and then stayed inside.
segments.s744-g45: دگر again 'another'; آنک is a relative particle 'that/which' introducing the description; نامش is 'its name' (نام + enclitic ش); همی‌بشنوی is the archaic present second‑person form 'you hear' (همی‑ marking continuous/habitual aspect), so this clause sets up a reaction one has on hearing the treasure's name.
segments.s744-g46: تو گویی here is an idiomatic verbless or semi‑verbal formula meaning 'you would say' or 'it seems (to you)'; همه 'all' modifies دیبه (diabeh) 'a rich brocade or silk (robe)', and خسروی is an adjective 'royal/kingly'. The phrase expresses that upon hearing the name one imagines the object as entirely royal brocade — гlossing the simile 'you would say it is all royal cloth'.
segments.s744-g47: دگر 'another' introduces this item; نامور is an adjective 'renowned' modifying گنج 'treasure', and افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' placed after گنج in this poetic apposition: 'the famed treasure of Afrasiab.'
segments.s744-g48: که is the relative 'which/that'; کس را with the negated past نبودی ('there was not / there did not exist') yields the idiom 'there was no one' (i.e., 'no one existed') and به خشکی و آب 'on land and water' gives the domain (land or sea). Together the clause says that no one on land or sea had/possessed or rivaled it (elliptical comparison typical in epic lists).
segments.s744-g17: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." برخیزد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive-form verb (classical style) meaning "rises/ is raised" or "sounds" and governs the following noun phrase. آواز طبل رحیل is a genitive/compound noun phrase: آواز (the sound/voice) is the head, modified by طبل رحیل ("the drum of departure"), where رحیل is a poetic noun meaning "departure."
segments.s744-g18: به خاک اندر آید is a literary locative phrase: به خاک + اندر آید literally "into the earth enters/comes," with اندر آید (comes/enters) as the verb. The nominal phrase سر مور وپیل ("the head of ant and elephant") follows in poetic inversion as the logical subject; وپیل is orthographically fused here (و + پیل = "and elephant").
segments.s744-g19: ز پرویز marks source/topic "about/from Parviz." چون here functions as a comparative/qualifying particle meaning roughly "such/so" (i.e., "a tale such as this"). داستانی is the noun "a tale/story" and شگفت is an adjective "wondrous/strange" modifying it.
segments.s744-g20: ز من بشنوی uses a subordinate verb form بشنوی (second-person present/subjunctive) with ز من "from me" = "if/when you hear from me." یاد باید گرفت is an impersonal necessity construction: باید (it is necessary) + گرفت (take) literally "a remembrance/lesson must be taken," i.e. "one must learn/take a lesson."
segments.s744-g21: که introduces an explanatory clause ("that"). چندی is an adverbial "for a while/for some time." سزاواری is a predicate adjective/nominal participle meaning "deserving/worthy of" and دستگاه is the noun "court/establishment" governed by that predicate.
segments.s744-g22: This segment is a coordinated list of nouns (بزرگی و اورنگ و فر و سپاه) that are the things the court was "worthy of." The conjunction و links the items: بزرگی (greatness), اورنگ (throne/worldly glory), فر (farr — royal/divine glory), and سپاه (army).
segments.s744-g23: کزان (from/of which) begins a relative phrase. بیشتر (more/greatly) combines with نشنوی to form "you will not hear more" — here نشنوی has the negative prefix ن- ("do not / will not hear"), so the verb means "you will not hear"; در جهان specifies the scope "in the world."
segments.s744-g24: اگر چند پرسی is a conditional clause: اگر "if," چند here means "a little/some" (i.e. if you ask even a bit), and پرسی is second-person present/subjunctive "you ask." ز دانا مهان means "of/from the wise guests" (ز = from/about, دانا = wise, مهان = guests).
segments.s744-g25: This is a series introduced by the preposition ز (here 'from') listing places. Poetic وز between items is historically a contraction of و + ز ('and from'); word order is just a coordinated list of place‑names.
segments.s744-g26: ز introduces the following noun phrase 'هرکشوری' ('every country') as the source. کان is an archaic/poetic particle (here functioning like a relative/connector 'which/that') — its exact senses are not in the provided list. بد is an adjectival/adverbial modifier changing آباد to mean 'badly/poorly inhabited'; آباد بوم is a compound idea 'inhabited place/land' (آباد qualifying بوم).
segments.s744-g27: همی is a frequentative/continuous adverbial marking ongoing action with a past tense; باژ بردند is an idiomatic verb phrase 'they raised/let out a cry' (بردند here = 'took/uttered' in a verbalized sense). نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' following the verb — typical poetic word order.
segments.s744-g28: به governs a temporal/locative phrase here ('in' or 'during'); رخشنده modifies روز ('radiant day'). The coordinator و links the next nominal phrase: شبان سیاه — شبان is used poetically as 'nights' (this sense is not in the provided list), with سیاه 'black/dark' as its adjective.
segments.s744-g29: A simple coordination غلام و پرستنده ('servant and worshipper/devotee'). از هر دری is a prepositional phrase 'from every دری'; here دری is used in a broad/regional sense ('every quarter/sea/region') rather than the listed sense 'Dari (Persian)', so the closest provided sense is the maritime/region sense.
segments.s744-g30: This is a further list of sources introduced by repeated ز ('from'): ز در ('from the gate/door') و ز یاقوت ('and from ruby') و هر گوهری ('and every jewel'). Note the poetic repetition of the preposition and the coordination with و; در here means 'door/gate'.
segments.s744-g31: ز again lists the items ('from/of dinars and treasure'). کرانه نبود is a verbless existential negative construction meaning 'there was no limit/edge' (کرانه 'edge/limit', نبود 'was not'). Word order places the quantified items before the predicate 'کرانه نبود'.
segments.s744-g32: چنو is an archaic demonstrative/adverb meaning 'such' or 'so', qualifying خسرو used here generically ('a king like Khosrow' / 'such a sovereign as Khosrow'). اندر زمانه نبود is a locative temporal phrase 'was not in the time/age' with نبود = past negative 'did not exist.'
segments.s744-g49: دگر 'another' appears before the noun for contrast; گنج‌کش is a compound formed by گنج + کش (agentive '-kesh'), meaning 'one who draws/takes treasure'. خواندی is 2nd‑person past of خواندن here used as 'you called/summoned/named'. سوخته is an adjective (past participle) 'burned/scorched' qualifying the named person; Persian allows such adjectival epithets after the verb phrase in poetic compression.
segments.s744-g50: کزان is a relative prepositional compound 'from which' that introduces a clause giving the source; گنج is 'treasure' (the thing 'from which'); بد modifies کشور and here should be read as an adverse adjective ('bad/ill/ill‑fated') describing the realm (کشور); افروخته is a past‑participle/adjective 'kindled, set ablaze' predicated of that realm (i.e. the treasure caused the realm to blaze).
segments.s744-g51: دگر 'another' again marks contrast; آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'that (one) / who' referencing the person; بد here is an archaic element that is difficult to pin down (it can occasionally appear as an intensifier or negative in classical use) and in context functions to modify شادورد; شادورد is a compound noun 'joy‑bringer' (one who brings gladness); بزرگ is the adjective 'great' following the noun as an epithet.
segments.s744-g52: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; گویند is the impersonal/formulaic 'they say' (classical present used to report a name or epithet); رامشگران is a plural noun 'comforters/peacemakers' (here the name or professional group being referenced, often musicians in this poetic context); سترگ is an epithet 'mighty/great' attached to that group (an older adjective in this usage).
segments.s744-g53: به is the preposition 'with/on' marking the material; زر سرخ is the noun + adjective 'red (rose) gold' (adjective follows noun in Persian); گوهر 'gem/jewel' names the ornament placed on the metal; برو is a compressed/poetic adverbial element (here best read as 'on it' / 'thereon' fused with the following participle); بافته is the past participle 'woven' describing the way the gems/ornamentation are worked into the metal or fabric.
segments.s744-g54: به زر اندرون is another material/location phrase 'with/in gold inside' (به + زر + اندرون = 'with gold within'); رشته‌ها is the plural 'threads/strands' (the things being woven); تافته is a past participle/adjectival perfect 'woven' (lit. 'they had woven'), completing the description of the workmanship. The structure is compact: prepositional phrase + plural subject + participle.
segments.s744-g55: ز is the poetic genitive/prepositional 'from' (short form of از); رامشگران is the plural noun 'comforters/peacemakers' (in context the name of a group of musicians); سرکش is an adjective 'headstrong/rebellious' modifying رامشگران; و connects the following proper name باربد (Barbad), a famous court musician, joining him to the group.
segments.s744-g56: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'; هرگز is the negative adverb 'never' placed before the verb for emphasis; نگشتی is 2nd‑person past/perfect of گشتن 'to become/turn' here used idiomatically with به to mean 'you did not turn/yield to' or 'you were never moved by'; به is the preposition 'to/toward'; آواز بد 'a bad/poor song/tune' is the object of yielding — i.e. 'you never yielded to a bad tune.'
segments.s744-g57: This phrase strings descriptive elements before the counted noun that follows: به + مشکوی زرین = a prepositive adjectival cluster meaning “musk‑scented, golden.” In poetry the numeral (ده و دوهزار) is placed with the adjectives rather than immediately before the noun; here ده و دوهزار reads as “two thousand and ten” (classical ordering: ‘ten and two thousand’).
segments.s744-g58: کنیزک is the counted noun “maidservant / little concubine.” The following prepositional phrase به کردار + X means “in the manner/character of X”; کردار is “manner/conduct.” خرم بهار is a descriptive simile — literally “joyful/pleasant spring” — so به کردار خرم بهار = “in the manner of the joyous spring.”
segments.s744-g59: دگر precedes the noun as an adverbial/quantifying particle meaning “another / a further.” پیل is the noun “elephant,” followed by the adjective بد (here used in the ordinary adjectival sense “bad/ill/fierce” right after the noun). The numeral phrase دو هزار و دویست follows to give the count (two thousand two hundred).
segments.s744-g60: که introduces a subordinate (reported/relative) clause: “that / as.” گفتی here functions as “you said / as you said” (idiomatic in classical Persian). ازان = “of that / of them,” and بر زمین جای نیست is a fixed phrase meaning “there is no place on earth” (بر زمین = on the ground/earth; جای نیست = there is no place).
segments.s744-g61: This is a simple list joined by و: فغستان (a place-name), چینی (the Chinese), پیل (elephants) and سپاه (an army/host). The items are coordinated as a catalogue of peoples and forces.
segments.s744-g62: که begins a relative clause modifying the preceding list: “which…” بر زین زرین = “upon a golden saddle” (بر = on/upon, زین = saddle, زرین = golden). بدی here is an archaic/poetic form used as a verb meaning essentially “you put/placed / caused to be” (classical texts often use short forms); سال و ماه = “years and months” gives the duration. The clause as a whole says these forces were placed on golden saddles for years and months.
segments.s744-g63: دگر again marks “another” group. اسب جنگی = “war‑horse” (اسب = horse; جنگی postposes the adjective ‘warlike’ / ‘for war’). ده و شش هزار here is a split poetic numeral (read together with the following segment) and contributes to the full count; in context the sequence yields the total “six thousand two hundred and ten.”
segments.s744-g64: دو صد = “two hundred.” بارگی here denotes burden‑bearing animals or the quality of being pack‑carriers (i.e. packhorses); کان نبد در شمار is an archaic idiom: کان + نبد (‘was not / did not exist’) + در شمار (“in the count”) — together meaning “were not included in the tally.” So this segment removes two hundred pack‑animals from the official count.
segments.s744-g65: ده و دو هزار is a compound numeral: ده و دو = twelve, followed by هزار = thousand, so the phrase reads “twelve thousand.” The conjunction و links the unit parts as in modern Persian numerals. اشتر بارکش is a noun phrase describing those animals: اشتر (camel) is the head noun and بارکش (‘load-bearing’/‘pack-’) is a descriptive element (a participial/agentive adjective) attached after it, yielding “pack-camels.”
segments.s744-g66: This segment continues to qualify the camels. عماری + کش contains the suffix -کش (agentive/‘-carrying’) attached to عماری, yielding “carrier of عماری” (i.e. a house/building–carrier or carrier of heavy loads for buildings); -کش is the agentive use of کش rather than the verb “to pull.” وگام زن should be read as و گام‌زن: و = and; گام‌زن is a compound participial adjective (گام + زن) meaning “pace‑walking” or “step‑striding” (one that marches/strides). شست وشش is a poetic/archaic numeral sequence corresponding to شصت و شش = 66 (old ordering/orthography appears here), giving the numeric count associated with the described group. The whole segment thus apposes further descriptions to the camels and gives the count “sixty‑six.”
segments.s744-g67: که introduces a clause (‘that’/‘which’). هرگز کس اندر جهان آن ندید is a negative past statement with the verb at the end: هرگز = never, کس here functions in a negative sense ‘no one’ (in combination with هرگز), اندر جهان = in the world, آن = that (the thing just described), and ندید is the past tense ‘saw’ (negated by the preceding context). Word order is standard classical Persian: negation/quantifier + subject + locative phrase + object + past verb.
segments.s744-g68: نه here continues the negation (‘nor’). از marks the source/agent (‘by’/‘from’), پیر سر کاردانان is a genitival/appositive string meaning ‘the old heads/chiefs of the experts’ — پیر = old/elder, سر used as ‘head/chief’ (leader), کاردانان = the skilled/experienced ones, and شنید is the past tense ‘heard’ (or ‘learned of’). Together: ‘nor did the elder chiefs of the experts hear of it.’
segments.s744-g69: چنویی (from چنو/چنین) = ‘in such a way’/‘thus’ and functions adverbially. به دست is a locative/benefactive phrase ‘into the hand of/into the charge of’; یکی here is ‘a certain’ (an indefinite pronoun) and پیشکار is the noun ‘attendant’/‘steward’. The whole chunk means ‘in such a way it came into the hand of a certain steward.’
segments.s744-g70: تبه شد is a two-word predicate: تبه (misfortune/ruin) + شد (past of شدن) meaning ‘it went wrong’/‘it came to ruin’ or more literally ‘mischief happened.’ The next clause addresses the addressee (تو = ‘you’) followed by an admonition in jussive/imperative style: تیمار و تنگی مدار = ‘do not (let) care and distress (be on you).’ مدار here is a poetic/archaic negative‑imperative/ jussive form (used to mean ‘do not have’/‘do not show’); the phrase admonishes against anxiety. Word order places the vocative/subject before the prohibition and the verb in its short poetic form at the end.
segments.s744-g71: This is an imperative instruction: تو = ‘you’ (vocative), بی + رنجی = ‘without toil/suffering’ (بی marks absence), از کارها = ‘from the tasks/affairs’, and برگزین is the imperative ‘choose!’ The prepositional phrase بی رنجی از کارها modifies the object of برگزین: ‘choose from your affairs the ones that involve no hardship.’
segments.s744-g72: چو here functions as a conditional ‘if’/‘when’ (archaic/poetic usage), خواهی = ‘you wish/want’ (present/subjunctive sense), که introduces the subordinate clause, and یابی is a present/subjunctive form ‘you may find/obtain.’ بداد آفرین is an idiomatic/poetic phrase: in context it refers to what the Creator gives or to divine justice/gift; both بداد and آفرین are somewhat archaic here and the exact lexical senses in the supplied list do not match perfectly, so they should be read together as pointing to the Creator’s just giving. The clause therefore means ‘if you wish to obtain (it) by the Creator’s justice/grant.’

sentences.s744-s17: From hawks and from goshawks and winged eagles; from lions and leopards and whales in the water.
sentences.s744-s18: All accepted his covenant; his soul shone like the bright sun.
sentences.s744-s19: The first — who laid down the bride's treasure — from China and from a wineskin-container, and from Rome and Rus.
sentences.s744-s20: Another treasure, full inside, stood in a pleasant spring; above it there was one thrown arrow (a projectile).
sentences.s744-s37: As good and evil pass through the world, Time counts our breath.
sentences.s744-s38: If you attain a throne, if (you attain) a crown and treasure, or even if for a while you are a seeker, you will be in hardship.
sentences.s744-s39: In the end your place will be ash and brick; you should plant nothing but the seed of goodness.
sentences.s744-s5: Do not be insolent toward the world (fate), for its poison is greater than any poison.
sentences.s744-s6: By no means, with greed and with malice lay not your hand on a dwelling; do not make it your settled place.
sentences.s744-s7: The house is 'sepang' in its ways and aspect; you are an old thing — others bring the new.
sentences.s744-s8: One enters, another passes by; for a time at the lodging the porter pauses (chews/rests).
sentences.s744-s1: Now I will speak of Khosrow's greatness; I will renew the old tale.
sentences.s744-s2: No one in the world has such greatness to be remembered from ancient times and the great.
sentences.s744-s3: Whoever reads that kingly book must cast his skirt from the world.
sentences.s744-s4: If it be fitting, let me tell one tale that a wise man may be of the same mind.
sentences.s744-s21: which they set as Khazra and named it Radan — those same Arabs, famed among the wise.
sentences.s744-s22: Another treasure they called 'Wind‑Bringer'; they counted it and remained inside.
sentences.s744-s23: Another — whose name, when you hear it, you would say is all royal brocade.
sentences.s744-s24: Another: the famed treasure of Afrasiab, which no one on land or sea possessed.
sentences.s744-s9: When the drum of departure sounds, the heads of ants and elephants sink into the earth.
sentences.s744-s10: If you hear from me a wondrous tale about Parviz, you must learn (a lesson).
sentences.s744-s11: That for a while the court deserved greatness, the throne, farr (royal glory), and an army.
sentences.s744-s12: You will not hear of any greater (thing) in the world, if you ask the wise guests even a little.
sentences.s744-s13: From Turan, from India, from China and Rome — from every country whose land was ill‑inhabited.
sentences.s744-s14: They continually raised cries near the king, in the radiant day and the black nights.
sentences.s744-s15: Servants and worshippers from every quarter — from gates and from rubies and every jewel.
sentences.s744-s16: There was no limit to dinars and treasure; such a sovereign as Khosrow did not exist in that age.
sentences.s744-s25: You then named another the treasure‑drawer, the Burned; from whose treasure the land was set ablaze.
sentences.s744-s26: Another — that one, the great bringer of joy; whom they call the mighty Ramashgarān.
sentences.s744-s27: Gems were set on red gold, and within it threads were woven of gold.
sentences.s744-s28: From the unruly Ramashgarān and Barbad — for you never yielded to a poor (bad) tune.
sentences.s744-s29: Two thousand and ten golden, musk‑scented maidservants, in the manner of the joyous spring.
sentences.s744-s30: Another two thousand two hundred elephants; as you said, of them there is no place on the earth.
sentences.s744-s31: Faghestān, the Chinese, elephants and a host — which you set upon golden saddles for years and months.
sentences.s744-s32: Another batch of war‑horses — six thousand two hundred and ten in all — and two hundred pack‑animals that were not included in the count.
sentences.s744-s33: Twelve thousand pack-camels — sixty-six of them house-carriers and pace-striders.
sentences.s744-s34: That no one in the world ever saw that, nor did the oldest of the experienced men hear of it.
sentences.s744-s35: Thus it came into the hand of a certain steward; it went ill — do not distress yourself with care and anguish.
sentences.s744-s36: You, choose from your tasks those free of toil — if you wish to obtain (it) by the Creator’s justice.
